CheckTitle: AI Agent Must Use OIDC/Workload Identity Federation Instead of Static Credentials
CheckType: Software and Configuration Checks/AI Agent Security Best Practices
Description: **AI Agent Identity**. Confirms that AI agents authenticate to cloud providers using OIDC Workload Identity Federation or a platform-managed identity (IAM role for EC2, Managed Identity, GCP Service Account with Workload Identity) rather than static API keys or access tokens. Static credentials are the leading cause of cloud security incidents.
Risk: Static credentials stored in environment variables, configuration files, or secrets managers can be exfiltrated through prompt injection, SSRF attacks targeting the IMDS endpoint, supply chain compromises, or insider threats. Once exfiltrated, static credentials provide persistent access until manually revoked.

Code.Other:
1. AWS: Assign an IAM role to the EC2 instance, ECS task, Lambda function, or EKS pod running the agent. Remove all static AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY environment variables.
2. GCP: Attach a service account to the Compute instance or GKE workload and enable Workload Identity for GKE.
3. Azure: Enable System-Assigned or User-Assigned Managed Identity on the compute resource and remove application secrets.
4. On-premises / multi-cloud: Configure OIDC federation so the agent's CI/CD or runtime can exchange short-lived OIDC tokens for cloud credentials.

Recommendation.Text: Migrate all AI agents to OIDC Workload Identity Federation or platform-managed identities. Eliminate static credentials entirely and enforce this through IaC policy as code.

Notes: ASPM-011 check from the Prowler ASPM check suite.
